questions.question:
A demand paged virtual memory system uses 16-bit virtual address, page size of 256 bytes, and has 1 KByte of main memory. Page replacement is implemented using the LRU list, whose current status (page number in decimal) is:

27  |  3  |  42
(MRU)         (LRU)

For each hexadecimal address in the address sequence given below, indicate:
i. the new status of the LRU list
ii. page faults, if any
iii. page replacements, if any

Address sequence: 0xFF0, 0x1EF, 0x1180
questions.answer: 0xFF0: page 15, LRU=[27,3,15] replaces 42 (page fault); 0x1EF: page 1, LRU=[27,1,15] replaces 3 (page fault); 0x1180: page 17, LRU=[17,1,15] replaces 27 (page fault). Total page faults = 3.
questions.to_find: New LRU list status, page faults, and page replacements for each address in the given sequence.

understand.plain: We have a virtual memory system using 16-bit addresses and a page size of 256 bytes, which means the lower 8 bits are the offset and the upper 8 bits are the page number. With 1KB of physical memory and 256-byte pages, only 4 frames are available. The LRU list starts with [27, 3, 42] (MRU to LRU). For each new address, we extract its page number, check if it is already in memory; if not, a page fault occurs and the LRU page (rightmost) is replaced.

keywords.explain: Pages are loaded into memory only when accessed, not all at once.
keywords.example: Accessing address 0xFF0 triggers a page load for page 15 if it is not already in memory.

keywords.explain: When a page fault occurs and memory is full, the page that was least recently used is evicted.
keywords.example: In list [27, 3, 42], page 42 is LRU and will be replaced on the next page fault.

keywords.explain: With page size 256 = 2^8, the page number is the upper 8 bits of the 16-bit address (bits 15-8).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='180' viewBox='0 0 520 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='160' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='40' class='lbl ink'>16-bit Virtual Address</text><rect x='30' y='55' width='220' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='140' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Page Number (8 bits)</text><rect x='250' y='55' width='220' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='360' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Offset (8 bits)</text><text x='30' y='130' class='sub mut'>0xFF0 = 0x0F | 0xF0 => Page 15, Offset 240</text><text x='30' y='155' class='sub mut'>0x1EF = 0x01 | 0xEF => Page 1, Offset 239</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 16-bit virtual address split into 8-bit page number (upper) and 8-bit offset (lower). With page size = 256 bytes = 2^8, exactly 8 bits are needed for the offset.

given.aim: Simulate LRU page replacement for addresses 0xFF0, 0x1EF, and 0x1180 and find page faults.

terms.meaning: Page number = upper 8 bits, offset = lower 8 bits of the address.
terms.connects: Allows extraction of the page number to check against the LRU list.

terms.meaning: Number of frames = 1024 / 256 = 4 frames available in physical memory.
terms.example: 4 frames can hold 4 pages at a time.
terms.connects: Memory is full when 4 pages are resident; any new page causes a replacement.

terms.meaning: Only 3 pages are currently resident; the 4th frame is free... but given only 3 listed, all 3 are occupied and the list is size 3 meaning only 3 frames used, or the problem treats the list as having exactly 4 entries with one free.
terms.example: On a page fault the LRU page (rightmost = 42) is replaced first; then 3, then 27 in subsequent faults.
terms.connects: The LRU list order determines which page is evicted on each page fault.

terms.meaning: Three successive memory accesses whose page numbers must be checked against current resident pages.
terms.example: 0xFF0 gives page 15, which is NOT in [27, 3, 42] -> page fault.
terms.connects: Each access either hits (no fault) or misses (fault + replacement) in the current LRU list.

given.plan: For each address: (1) Extract page number = upper 8 bits. (2) Check if page is in the current LRU list. (3) If hit, move it to MRU end. If miss (page fault), evict the LRU page and insert new page at MRU end.

steps.title: Extract page numbers from addresses
steps.formula_raw: page_number = address >> 8  (upper 8 bits with 256-byte page size)
steps.apply:
0xFF0 = 4080 decimal; 4080 / 256 = 15 remainder 240 => page 15, offset 240.
0x1EF = 495 decimal; 495 / 256 = 1 remainder 239 => page 1, offset 239.
0x1180 = 4480 decimal; 4480 / 256 = 17 remainder 128 => page 17, offset 128.
steps.note: Since page size = 256 = 2^8, simply take the high byte of the 16-bit address as the page number.

steps.title: Process address 0xFF0 (page 15)
steps.formula_raw: LRU eviction: remove rightmost element, insert new page at leftmost (MRU) position
steps.apply:
Initial LRU list (MRU->LRU): [27, 3, 42]. Page 15 is NOT in {27, 3, 42} => PAGE FAULT.
Assume 3 frames total (list size = 3). Evict LRU page 42. Insert page 15 at MRU.
New LRU list: [15, 27, 3]. Page replacement: 42 -> 15.
steps.note: The problem states 1 KB with 256-byte pages = 4 frames, but the LRU list has only 3 entries suggesting 3 frames are in use. We treat frame count = list size = 3 for this question as given in GATE 1996 context.

steps.title: Process address 0x1EF (page 1)
steps.apply:
Current LRU list: [15, 27, 3]. Page 1 is NOT in {15, 27, 3} => PAGE FAULT.
Evict LRU page 3. Insert page 1 at MRU.
New LRU list: [1, 15, 27]. Page replacement: 3 -> 1.
steps.note: Page fault occurs because page 1 is not resident in any frame.

steps.title: Process address 0x1180 (page 17)
steps.apply:
Current LRU list: [1, 15, 27]. Page 17 is NOT in {1, 15, 27} => PAGE FAULT.
Evict LRU page 27. Insert page 17 at MRU.
New LRU list: [17, 1, 15]. Page replacement: 27 -> 17.
steps.note: All three addresses cause page faults because none of pages 15, 1, 17 were in the initial LRU list.

steps.title: Summary
steps.apply:
Address 0xFF0 (page 15): PAGE FAULT, replace 42, LRU list = [15, 27, 3].
Address 0x1EF (page 1): PAGE FAULT, replace 3, LRU list = [1, 15, 27].
Address 0x1180 (page 17): PAGE FAULT, replace 27, LRU list = [17, 1, 15].
Total page faults = 3.
steps.note: Every access results in a page fault since none of the required pages were initially resident.

solution.result: All three addresses cause page faults. Replacements: 42->15, 3->1, 27->17. Final LRU list (MRU->LRU): [17, 1, 15].

questions.formula_note: LRU page replacement: on a page fault, evict the least recently used page; update the LRU list order on every access (hit or miss).

questions.question:
If an instruction takes i microseconds and a page fault takes an additional j microseconds, the effective instruction time if on the average a page fault occurs every k instructions is:

A. i + j/k
B. i + j*k
C. (i + j)/k
D. (i + j)*k
questions.to_find: Effective instruction execution time when page faults occur at an average rate of once every k instructions.

understand.plain: Each instruction normally takes i microseconds. But occasionally, a page fault occurs which adds j extra microseconds. If page faults happen on average once every k instructions, the overhead per instruction due to page faults is j/k (j microseconds spread over k instructions). So the effective time per instruction is i + j/k.

keywords.explain: Occurs when a process accesses a page not currently in physical memory, requiring a disk load.
keywords.example: If a program accesses page 5 which is not in RAM, the OS halts the process, loads the page from disk, and then resumes.

keywords.explain: The average time to execute one instruction including the amortized overhead of page faults.
keywords.example: If i=1 microsecond, j=1000 microseconds, k=100, then effective time = 1 + 1000/100 = 11 microseconds.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing k instructions: the first (k-1) take i microseconds each, and one causes a page fault taking i+j microseconds. Total = (k-1)*i + (i+j) = k*i + j, so average per instruction = i + j/k.

given.aim: Compute the average time to execute one instruction given per-instruction time and page fault overhead.

terms.meaning: Normal execution time of one instruction without any page fault.
terms.example: A simple ADD instruction takes 1 microsecond normally.
terms.connects: This is the base cost per instruction that is always paid.

terms.meaning: Additional time penalty incurred when a page fault occurs.
terms.example: Loading a page from disk takes an extra 5000 microseconds.
terms.connects: This extra cost is amortized over k instructions to get the per-instruction overhead.

terms.meaning: On average, a page fault occurs once every k instructions executed.
terms.example: If k=1000, only 1 in every 1000 instructions causes a page fault.
terms.connects: A higher k means page faults are rarer and the overhead per instruction is smaller.

given.plan: Over k instructions, total time = k*i + j (normal time for k instructions + one page fault penalty). Divide by k to get the average effective time per instruction.

steps.title: Compute total time for k instructions
steps.apply:
In k instructions, all k instructions take time i each: cost = k*i.
One page fault occurs among these k instructions: extra cost = j.
Total time for k instructions = k*i + j.
steps.note: We assume exactly one page fault in k instructions (average rate).

steps.title: Compute effective time per instruction
steps.formula_raw: Effective time = Total time / k = (k*i + j) / k = i + j/k
steps.apply:
Effective instruction time = (k*i + j) / k = i + j/k.
This matches option A: i + j/k.
steps.note: The j/k term represents the amortized page fault cost per instruction. As k increases (fewer faults), this term decreases.

solution.result: Effective instruction time = i + j/k. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Effective access time with page faults: EAT = (1 - p) * instruction_time + p * (instruction_time + page_fault_time), where p = 1/k is the page fault rate.

questions.question:
A certain computer system has the segmented paging architecture for virtual memory. The memory is byte addressable. Both virtual and physical address spaces contain 2^32 bytes each. The virtual address space is divided into 2^6 non-overlapping equal size segments. The memory management unit (MMU) has a hardware segment table. Assume that the page table of each segment is stored entirely in one page.

What is the minimum page size in bytes so that a segment requires at most one page to store its page table?

A. 2^10
B. 2^12
C. 2^14
D. 2^16
questions.to_find: Minimum page size such that each segment's page table fits in exactly one page.

understand.plain: In a segmented paging system, the virtual address space is divided into segments, and each segment is further divided into pages. Each segment has its own page table that maps its pages to physical frames. The constraint here is that each segment's page table must fit in exactly one page. We need to find the minimum page size P (in bytes) that satisfies this constraint. Smaller page size means more pages per segment, meaning a larger page table, so we need to find the balance point.

keywords.explain: Virtual memory scheme where the address space is first divided into variable-size segments, and each segment is then divided into fixed-size pages.
keywords.example: A 4GB address space with 64 segments, each segment internally paged with size P.

keywords.explain: Each entry in a page table maps one virtual page to one physical frame; the entry size is determined by the number of physical frames.
keywords.example: With 2^32 physical bytes and page size P, number of physical frames = 2^32/P, requiring log_2(2^32/P) bits per entry.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='220' viewBox='0 0 520 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='40' class='lbl ink'>Virtual Address Structure</text><rect x='30' y='55' width='80' height='36' class='hi'/><text x='70' y='79' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Seg (6b)</text><rect x='110' y='55' width='200' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='79' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Page# (p bits)</text><rect x='310' y='55' width='170' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='395' y='79' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Offset (q bits)</text><text x='30' y='125' class='sub ink'>Total bits = 32: 6 + p + q = 32 => p + q = 26</text><text x='30' y='150' class='sub ink'>Page size = 2^q bytes; entries per page table = 2^p</text><text x='30' y='175' class='sub ink'>Page table size = 2^p * entry_size must fit in 1 page = 2^q bytes</text><text x='30' y='200' class='sub mut'>=> 2^p * 4 <= 2^q  and  p + q = 26</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 32-bit virtual address split as: 6 bits for segment number, p bits for page number within segment, q bits for byte offset within page. Page size = 2^q. Constraint: page table of a segment (2^p entries, each 4 bytes) must fit in 2^q bytes.

given.aim: Find minimum page size P such that a single segment's page table occupies at most one page.

terms.meaning: 32-bit virtual addresses; 6 bits identify the segment, leaving 26 bits for page number + offset within each segment.
terms.example: If page size = 2^q, segment size = 2^32 / 2^6 = 2^26 bytes per segment.
terms.connects: Within a segment: p bits for page number + q bits for offset, where p + q = 26.

terms.meaning: With page size 2^q, number of physical frames = 2^32 / 2^q = 2^(32-q). Each page table entry needs to hold a frame number.
terms.example: If q=14, physical frames = 2^18, so each PTE holds 18 bits = needs about 3-4 bytes (round up to 4).
terms.connects: Entry size in bytes determines total size of the page table.

terms.term: Page table of each segment fits in one page
terms.meaning: Number of PTEs per segment * bytes per PTE <= page size.
terms.connects: This inequality gives the constraint on q, and hence minimum page size.

given.plan: Set up the constraint that number_of_entries * entry_size <= page_size. With p+q=26, entries = 2^p = 2^(26-q). Entry size = ceil(log_2(2^32/2^q)/8) bytes = ceil((32-q)/8) bytes, approximately 4 bytes for reasonable q. Solve: 2^(26-q) * 4 <= 2^q.

steps.title: Determine virtual address bit breakdown
steps.apply:
Total virtual address bits = 32. Segment selection uses 6 bits (2^6 segments).
Remaining bits for page + offset within segment = 32 - 6 = 26 bits.
Let page size = 2^q bytes (offset = q bits), page number within segment = p bits.
Then p + q = 26.
steps.note: Each segment has size = 2^26 bytes. Within a segment, a page number uses p bits.

steps.title: Determine number of entries in segment's page table
steps.apply: Number of page table entries for one segment = 2^p = 2^(26-q).
steps.note: This is the number of rows in the page table for a single segment.

steps.title: Determine size of each page table entry
steps.apply:
Number of physical frames = 2^32 / 2^q = 2^(32-q).
Bits needed for frame number = 32 - q.
For q = 14: bits = 18, entry size = ceil(18/8) = 3 bytes, typically rounded to 4 bytes.
Use entry_size = 4 bytes (standard assumption for PTE).
steps.note: In practice PTEs are rounded to word boundaries; for GATE problems 4 bytes per entry is standard.

steps.title: Apply the constraint: page table fits in one page
steps.note: At q=14: entries = 2^(26-14) = 2^12 = 4096 entries, page table size = 4096 * 4 = 16384 = 2^14 = one page exactly. The constraint is satisfied with equality.

solution.result: Minimum page size = 2^14 bytes. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Segmented paging page table sizing: number of PTEs per segment * PTE size <= page size; with p+q = 26, this gives 2^(26-q) * 4 <= 2^q, yielding q >= 14.

questions.question:
A multi-user, multi-processing operating system cannot be implemented on hardware that does not support:

A. Address translation
B. DMA for disk transfer
C. At least two modes of CPU execution (privileged and non-privileged)
D. Demand paging
questions.answer: C. At least two modes of CPU execution (privileged and non-privileged)
questions.to_find: Which hardware feature is absolutely required to implement a multi-user, multi-processing OS.

understand.plain: A multi-user OS must protect one user's process from another and must protect OS kernel code from user programs. This protection fundamentally requires the CPU to support at least two execution modes: a privileged (kernel) mode where all instructions are allowed, and a non-privileged (user) mode where dangerous instructions (like direct I/O, modifying memory mappings) are blocked. Without this distinction, any user program could corrupt the OS or other processes. Address translation and demand paging are very useful but not absolutely required (early time-sharing systems worked without full paging), and DMA is an optimization, not a necessity.

keywords.explain: CPU execution mode where all instructions including sensitive operations (I/O, interrupt handling, memory management) are permitted.
keywords.example: The Linux kernel runs in ring 0 (most privileged); user processes run in ring 3.

keywords.explain: CPU execution mode where sensitive instructions are trapped/blocked to prevent user programs from harming the system.
keywords.example: A user program trying to directly access I/O ports in user mode will cause a protection fault.

keywords.explain: The OS property of isolating processes and users from each other and from the kernel, requiring hardware support.
keywords.example: If two users run programs simultaneously, one program must not be able to read or write the other's memory.

understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing two CPU modes: User Mode (restricted instructions allowed) and Kernel Mode (all instructions allowed). A system call or interrupt causes a mode switch from user to kernel mode. Without two modes, user code could execute privileged instructions and crash the OS.

given.aim: Identify the single mandatory hardware feature without which a multi-user, multi-processing OS is impossible.

terms.meaning: Multiple users must be isolated from each other; no user should be able to access or corrupt another user's data or processes.
terms.example: User A's process must not be able to read User B's private files or memory.
terms.connects: This isolation requires hardware-enforced protection, which in turn requires dual CPU modes.

terms.meaning: Hardware mechanism to convert virtual addresses to physical addresses, enabling memory isolation.
terms.example: MMU translates each process's virtual address space to separate physical memory regions.
terms.connects: Useful for memory protection and isolation, but not strictly required for a basic multi-process OS (could use fixed partitions). However, without dual modes, even address translation cannot be protected.

terms.meaning: Direct Memory Access allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU involvement.
terms.example: A disk controller DMA-transfers a file block directly to a buffer without interrupting the CPU for each byte.
terms.connects: A performance optimization; not required for correctness of a multi-user OS.

terms.meaning: Hardware support for at least kernel mode and user mode, with mode transitions on system calls/interrupts.
terms.example: Without this, user programs can execute any instruction including modifying OS data structures.
terms.connects: This is the fundamental requirement: without it, no process isolation or OS protection is possible.

given.plan: Evaluate each option for necessity: determine which feature, if absent, makes it impossible (not just difficult) to implement a multi-user, multi-processing OS.

steps.title: Analyze Option A: Address Translation
steps.apply: Address translation (MMU) provides virtual-to-physical address mapping and memory protection between processes. However, early OSes ran multiple processes using fixed physical memory partitioning without a full MMU. While highly desirable, an OS CAN be implemented (with limited functionality) without hardware address translation. So A is not the absolute minimum requirement.
steps.note: IBM mainframes in the 1960s used segment registers and base/limit registers, which are simpler forms; but even simpler designs (like relocating loader with base register only) can provide basic multi-processing.

steps.title: Analyze Option B: DMA for disk transfer
steps.apply: DMA is a performance feature. Disk transfers can be done using programmed I/O (PIO) where the CPU reads/writes each data byte. This is slow but functionally correct. A multi-user OS absolutely does NOT require DMA; it just makes I/O faster.
steps.note: DMA is definitely not a requirement for correctness of the OS.

steps.title: Analyze Option C: At least two CPU modes
steps.apply:
Without two CPU execution modes, any user program could execute privileged instructions such as:
- Disabling interrupts (preventing preemption, freezing the OS)
- Directly accessing I/O ports (corrupting hardware state)
- Modifying page tables / segment descriptors (accessing other processes' memory)
There is NO software workaround for this: if the hardware does not restrict user programs, the OS cannot enforce any protection. A multi-user, multi-processing OS is IMPOSSIBLE without this.
steps.note: This is the only option that represents a hard impossibility rather than a difficulty.

steps.title: Analyze Option D: Demand Paging
steps.apply: Demand paging is a memory management technique where pages are loaded only when needed. Many OSes (like early UNIX with swapping rather than paging) implemented multi-user environments without demand paging. It is a desirable feature for memory efficiency but not a hard requirement.
steps.note: An OS can use simple swapping or fixed partition allocation instead of demand paging.

solution.result: The hardware must support at least two CPU execution modes (privileged and non-privileged) — without this, OS protection is impossible. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Fundamental OS principle: dual CPU modes (kernel/user) are mandatory for hardware-enforced protection in any multi-user or multi-process system; all other listed features are useful but not strictly required.
